coredata.dc:title: Lanthanum ions assisted non-enzymatic ratiometric fluorescence probe for monitoring fenthion residues in agro-product samples
coredata.dc:creator: Guan, Mengting
He, Haibo
Li, Ruxing
Si, Xiaojing
Peng, Xitian
Yan, Xiaoxia
Yang, Zhaosheng
Nien, Enhsuan
Lei, Yunyi
Luo, Liqiang
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a La3+ assisted glutathione-capped gold nanoclusters and carbon dots (GSH-Au NCs/CDs) nanoplatform for sensitive detection of fenthion (FEN) is fabricated. The fluorescence response of GSH-Au NCs significantly increases due to aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE) effect induced by La3+, which is further enhanced with adding FEN due to the coordination between La3+ and FEN. Taking the fluorescence intensity of CDs as the signal background, the ratiometric fluorescence of GSH-Au NCs and CDs has a good linear relationship with the FEN concentration from 0.01 to 1.10 μg mL−1, and detecting FEN exhibits a good sensitivity at a low detection limit of 6.74 ng g−1. The La3+ assisted GSH-Au NCs/CDs nanoplatform demonstrates desirable selectivity and applicability for monitoring trace level of FEN in fruit and vegetable samples. The non-enzymatic strategy by taking advantage of successive AIEE of GSH-Au NCs has a great potential for facile screening organophosphate pesticides in agro-products.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Gold nanoclusters
La3+
Ratiometric fluorescence
Non-enzymatic probe
Fenthion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of FEN in real samples
Characterizations of CDs and GSH-Au NCs
The calibration curve for quantitative analysis of FEN
Application for the detection of FEN in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [grant numbers 21974085 , 61971274 ].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The UV–vis absorption spectrum, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CDs; the insets are the images of CDs under daylight (left) and 365 nm UV light (right). (B) The HRTEM image of CDs. (C) The FT-IR spectrum of CDs. (D) The UV–vis absorption spectrum, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of GSH-Au NCs; the insets are the images of GSH-Au NCs under daylight (left) and 365 nm UV light (right). (E) The HRTEM image of GSH-Au NCs. (F) The FT-IR spectra of GSH and GSH-Au NCs.

(A) The fluorescence emission spectra of CDs/GSH-Au NCs (curve a), CDs/GSH-Au NCs in the presence of 1.10 μg mL−1 FEN (curve b), 4 μmol L−1 La3+ (curve c), 4 μmol L−1 La3+ and 1.10 μg mL−1 FEN (curve d), respectively. The HRTEM images of (B) GSH-Au NCs in the absence of La3+, (C) GSH-Au NCs in the presence of 4 μmol L−1 La3+, (D) GSH-Au NCs in the presence of 4 μmol L−1 La3+ and 1.10 μg mL−1 FEN.

The effect of (A) volume of acetone, (B) concentration of La3+ in the system, (C) pH of FEN solution, (D) incubation time of La3+ with GSH-Au NCs and FEN on the fluorescence enhancement degree of GSH-Au NCs caused by FEN. The experimental conditions: 0.40 μg mL−1 FEN solution with 300 μL acetone, 80 μL GSH-Au NCs and 4 μmol L−1 La3+ (i.e. adding 50 μL of 160 μmol L−1 La3+) reacted at pH 4.5 for 30 min, following by the addition of 35 μL CDs.

(A) The fluorescence emission spectra of CDs/GSH-Au NCs in the presence of 4 μmol L−1 La3+ and different concentrations of FEN (from 0 to 1.10 μg mL−1), and the inset image is the corresponding image at 365 nm UV light. (B) The relationship plot of I606/I459 versus the concentration of FEN (from 0.01 to 1.10 μg mL−1).

The value of I606/I459 of (CDs/GSH-Au NCs + La3+) probe in the presence of FEN and various disrupting substances. The left bars represent the addition of individual disrupting substance and the right bars represent the addition of the mixture of FEN and individual disrupting substance. The concentration of FEN was 0.50 μg mL−1, and the concentration of each metal ion and pesticide was 10-fold and 5-fold of FEN, respectively.).
Schematic diagram of detection of FEN using (CDs/GSH-Au NCs + La3+) as the fluorescence probe.
Detection of FEN in real samples (n = 3).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: No signals were detected in apple, orange and cucumber blank samples.

head.ce:title: Lanthanum ions assisted non-enzymatic ratiometric fluorescence probe for monitoring fenthion residues in agro-product samples

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Agricultural Quality Standards and Testing Technology Research

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Agricultural Quality Standards and Testing Technology Research, Hubei Academy of Agricultural Sciences, Wuhan 430064, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a La3+ assisted glutathione-capped gold nanoclusters and carbon dots (GSH-Au NCs/CDs) nanoplatform for sensitive detection of fenthion (FEN) is fabricated. The fluorescence response of GSH-Au NCs significantly increases due to aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE) effect induced by La3+, which is further enhanced with adding FEN due to the coordination between La3+ and FEN. Taking the fluorescence intensity of CDs as the signal background, the ratiometric fluorescence of GSH-Au NCs and CDs has a good linear relationship with the FEN concentration from 0.01 to 1.10 μg mL−1, and detecting FEN exhibits a good sensitivity at a low detection limit of 6.74 ng g−1. The La3+ assisted GSH-Au NCs/CDs nanoplatform demonstrates desirable selectivity and applicability for monitoring trace level of FEN in fruit and vegetable samples. The non-enzymatic strategy by taking advantage of successive AIEE of GSH-Au NCs has a great potential for facile screening organophosphate pesticides in agro-products.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Organophosphate pesticides (OPs) are a kind of organic compounds containing organophosphate, which are widely applied in agriculture to protect crops against pests to improve the output of agricultural products and decrease losses [1]. Nevertheless, the excessive use of OPs causes severe residue problems in soil, water and farm products, which poses a serious threat to ecosystem and human health [2]. Many of these pesticides are highly toxic to humans and animals such as parathion, fenthion (FEN) and chlorpyrifos, which can damage the human nerve system, causing neurodegenerative diseases such as Alzheimer's disease and Parkinson disease as well as neurodevelopmental disorders such as attention deficit hyperactivity disorder and autism [3]. Consequently, it is important to establish sensitive and reliable analytical methods to monitor OPs residues in agricultural products to guarantee food safety.

At present, gas chromatography and high-performance liquid chromatography are well-accepted techniques for OPs analysis [4–6]. Nonetheless, these techniques are sometimes constrained by expensive mass spectrometry and tedious sample pretreatment prior to analysis [7,8]. As a widely used alternative, electrochemical methods exhibit the advantage of high sensitivity for detecting OPs, but they always rely on expensive organophosphorus hydrolase or acetylcholinesterase (AChE) and tedious process for the modification of electrodes [9]. Fluorimetric methods are attracting intense attention in pesticides monitoring due to the merits of intuitive visual signals, easy operation and quick response without sacrificing sensitivity and selectivity [10,11]. So far, many fluorescence assays mainly relied on the AChE-inhibition mechanism for the detection of OPs, namely, the activity of AChE was inhibited by OPs and the hydrolysis of acetylthiocholine was thus reduced in the presence of OPs [12]. For example, Yang et al. and Li et al. both proposed carbon dots (CDs) based fluorescence methods for detecting OPs through the AChE-inhibition mechanism [13,14]. Huang et al. proposed a Cu2+ involved ratiometric fluorescence method for detecting dichlorvos based on AChE-inhibition mechanism by using CDs and CdTe quantum dots as the sensing probe [15]. These enzyme involved fluorescence methods showed good sensitivity for the determination of OPs, however, enzyme reagents are always expensive and unsuitable for long-period preservation, and susceptible to temperature, organic solutions and pH [16]. Therefore, the fabrication of non-enzymatic fluorescence sensing methods for sensitive analysis of OPs in a cost-effective way would be very desirable.

Thiolate-protected metal nanoclusters have recently emerged as a promising class of functional materials in fluorescence analysis because of their unique aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE) property [17]. In the light of the principle of AIEE, an enhanced emission intensity of metal nanoclusters can be obtained in an aggregation state when the intramolecular rotations and vibrations of ligands are restricted [18,19], thus metal ions such as Al3+, Zn2+ and Ag + induced aggregation of gold nanoclusters (Au NCs) has been exploited for constructing AIEE based fluorescence methods. For instance, Al3+ induced AIEE effect of glutathione-capped Au NCs (GSH-Au NCs) was studied and applied for detecting ciprofloxacin by Fu et al. [20], and Zn2+ induced AIEE effect of Au NCs was applied for fluorescence sensing of pyrophosphate by Zhao et al. [21], and Ag+ induced AIEE phenomenon of GSH-Au NCs showed excellent performance for the detection of cysteine in human serum samples [22] and carbendazim residue in fruit samples [23], respectively. Moreover, Desai et al. found that the fluorescence intensity of bovine serum albumin-modified Au NCs (BSA-Au NCs) significantly enhanced when adding La3+ in the synthesis process of Au NCs [24].

As compared to fluorescence assays with single emission, the ratiometric fluorescence methods rely on measuring the ratio of fluorescence intensity at two different wavelengths in a sensing system as the signal parameter, which can overcome the signal fluctuation and provide more reliable and accurate analytical results [25–27]. Thus, the ratiometric fluorescence sensing mode is highly favored for constructing various fluorescence sensing methods. Moreover, CDs as an emerging fluorescence nanomaterial is usually used as reference in ratiometric fluorescence due to the advantages of low toxicity, facile synthesis and good solubility [28,29]. In light of the polydentate coordination sites of La3+ and high affinity of La3+ to oxygen atom, in this study, we proposed a CDs and GSH-Au NCs based non-enzymatic ratiometric fluorescence method for FEN detection by taking advantage of La3+ induced AIEE effect, in which the binding of La3+ with both GSH and FEN resulted in a successive fluorescence enhancement of GSH-Au NCs. The fluorescence intensity of GSH-Au NCs increased upon an increase of FEN concentration, while the fluorescence intensity of CDs was not affected, so FEN can be quantitatively determined through measuring the ratio of fluorescence intensity of GSH-Au NCs and CDs. This (CDs/GSH-Au NCs + La3+) based fluorescence platform demonstrates excellent selectivity towards FEN and good applicability for detecting FEN in apple, orange and cucumber samples, manifesting its great potential for monitoring FEN residues in agricultural products. To the best of our knowledge, no non-enzymatic ratiometric fluorescence method has been reported for monitoring of FEN in fruit and vegetable samples.

Gold chloride trihydrate (HAuCl4 3H2O), glutathione (GSH), citric acid, ethylenediamine, acetone, H3PO4, KH2PO4, K2HPO4, KCl, NaCl, CaCl2 and MgCl2 were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., China. LaCl3⋅7H2O was purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd., China. FEN, chlorothalonil, vinclozolin, bifenthrin and fenpropathrin standard solutions were obtained from Agro-Environmental Protection Institute (Tianjin, China).

All reagents were analytically pure and used without further purification. Ultrapure water was used in all experiments. All the glass wares used in the experiment were fully soaked in aqua regia, then rinsed using ultrapure water, and dried before use. The FEN standard stock solution (1000 μg mL−1) was diluted to 10 μg mL−1 with acetone, and then was diluted with 0.01 mol L−1 phosphate buffer salines (PBS, pH 4.5) to obtained the FEN solutions with various concentrations (from 0.01 to 1.10 μg mL−1).

The fluorescence spectra were recorded on fluorescence spectrophotometer (RF-5301PC, Shimadzu Corporation, Japan). The UV–vis absorption spectra were taken by spectrophotometer (UV–2501PC, Shimadzu Corporation, Japan). The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of fluorescence probe were obtained on FT-IR spectrometer (AVATAR370, Thermo Nicolet Corporation, USA). The morphology structures of GSH-Au NCs and CDs were taken by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) (JEM-2100 F, Japan Electron Optics Laboratory Co., Ltd).

The CDs were prepared according to the previous reports [30]. In brief, 1.26 g citric acid was dissolved in 30 mL ultrapure water and 1.6 mL ethylenediamine was added, then the mixture was placed in a 50 mL Teflon lined stainless-steel autoclave. The reaction was conducted at 180 °C for 5 h, then cooled down naturally to room temperature. The obtained solution was purified using a dialysis bag (MW 500) for 24 h. Finally, the resultant samples were dried at 60 °C in a vacuum drying oven and used for preparing the stock solution (10.0 mg mL−1) of CDs.

GSH-Au NCs were prepared in accordance with previous reports [31,32]. In brief, 4.0 mL HAuCl4 (20 mmol L−1) was added into 34.8 mL ultrapure water at room temperature, followed by the addition of 1.2 mL GSH (100 mmol L−1). Then the above mixture solution was incubated at 70 °C for 24 h with slow stirring. Finally, the obtained GSH-Au NCs solution emitted strong orange fluorescence and was stored at 4 °C.

A range of FEN working solutions with various concentrations (from 0.01 to 1.10 μg mL−1) were obtained by diluting the stock solution of FEN with 300 μL acetone and 1535 μL PBS (0.01 mol L−1, pH 4.5) and mixed evenly under vortex. Then, 80 μL GSH-Au NCs and 50 μL La3+ (160 μmol L−1) were added to the above 1835 μL solution in sequence. After the mixture was incubated at 25 °C for 30 min, 35 μL CDs (0.2 mg mL−1) was added. A uniform individual solution was obtained by ultrasound and then was transferred to a quartz cell before testing. The fluorescence spectra were measured at the excitation wavelength of 410 nm. The ratios of fluorescence intensity of GSH-Au NCs and CDs (I606/I459) were recorded as the quantitative criterion for FEN determination. Herein, I606 and I459 represented the fluorescence intensity of sensing system at 606 nm and 459 nm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of FEN in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Apple, orange and cucumber were bought at local supermarket, all of which were homogenized with a juice blender before used for analysis. The spiked samples with a low (0.015 μg g−1), medium (0.15 μg g−1) and high (0.75 μg g−1) concentration of FEN were prepared by adding 300 μL FEN standard solutions with a concentration ranged from 0.5 μg mL−1 to 25 μg mL−1 into 10 g homogenized samples. The individual spiked samples were vortex-mixed with 10 mL dichloromethane for 2 min, then the mixture solutions were incubated at 50 °C for 10 min after removing the insoluble substances. The residues were redissolved with 2.45 mL acetone and diluted with 0.01 mol L−1 PBS (pH 4.5) to 15 mL. The extraction solutions with spiked FEN concentration of 0.01 μg mL−1, 0.10 μg mL−1 and 0.50 μg mL−1 were analyzed using the as-developed fluorescence method as described in section 2.4. Blank samples without spiking FEN were also analyzed. The recoveries of spiked samples were evaluated by calculating the ratios of concentration of FEN obtained from the calibration curve to the spiked concentration of FEN.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of CDs and GSH-Au NCs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1 A, the synthesized CDs emitted an intense blue fluorescence under UV light (λ = 365 nm), and has a UV absorption peak at 350 nm and an emission peak at 447 nm under the excitation wavelength of 355 nm. The HRTEM image in Fig. 1B showed that CDs displayed a uniform and monodisperse spherical structure with ultrasmall size of 2 ± 0.5 nm. Fig. 1C displayed that the FT-IR absorption peaks of CDs at 3425 cm−1, 2832 cm−1, 1602 cm−1, 1363 cm−1 and 1076 cm−1, which were ascribed to the stretching vibrations of O–H/N–H, C–H, CO, C–O and C–N, respectively [33]. The FT-IR results indicated that there were abundant carboxyl and amino groups on the surface of CDs. The as-synthesized CDs are stable and there is no obvious change in fluorescence intensity when stored at 4 °C in the dark for a few months.

Fig. 1D showed that the obtained GSH-Au NCs solution presented as a pale yellow and transparent liquid under daylight and emitted bright orange fluorescence under UV light (λ = 365 nm), and there was a strong UV absorption peak at around 375 nm for GSH-Au NCs, and the fluorescence spectra of GSH-Au NCs displayed two emission peaks at 606 nm and 642 nm and an excitation peak at 430 nm. As presented in Fig. 1E, GSH-Au NCs exhibited well monodisperse spheres and possessed ultrasmall size with a mean size of around 3.5 ± 0.5 nm. The lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.235 nm were clearly visible in the inset in Fig. 1E, revealing that GSH-Au NCs matched well with the plane of Au (111) [34]. No characteristic absorption of S–H at 2522 cm−1 could be observed in the FT-IR spectrum of GSH-Au NCs in Fig. 1F, implying that there was no existence of free GSH in GSH-Au NCs solution due to the formation of Au–S bond [35]. The as-prepared GSH-Au NCs can be well-kept at 4 °C in the dark for a few months without decreasing the fluorescence intensity.

Fig. S1A and Fig. S1B displayed the fluorescence emission spectra of CDs and GSH-Au NCs under various excitation wavelengths, respectively. With an increase of excitation wavelength from 310 nm to 410 nm at an interval of 10 nm, the fluorescence intensity of CDs increased first and then decreased, and reached a maximum value when the excitation wavelength was 350 nm (as shown in Fig. S1A). The fluorescence intensity of GSH-Au NCs gradually declined with an increase of excitation wavelength from 380 nm to 470 nm at an interval of 10 nm (as shown in Fig. S1B). Neither of the emission peaks of CDs and GSH-Au NCs shifted with increasing the excitation wavelength, demonstrating that the emissions of CDs and GSH-Au NCs were both excitation-independent, which may attribute to their uniform size distributions as reflected in Fig. 1B and E [36,37]. In this CDs/GSH-Au NCs system, the excitation wavelength was set at 410 nm with a slit width at 5 nm, to allow GSH-Au NCs to emit bright as well as reduce the blue background resulting from CDs.

As shown in Fig. S1C, three emission peaks at 459 nm, 606 nm and 642 nm appeared in fluorescence emission spectrum of the mixture of CDs and GSH-Au NCs, and there was only a slight peak shift change for the emission peaks of CDs and GSH-Au NCs in the mixture. It can be concluded that fluorescent properties of CDs and GSH-Au NCs were not affected by mixing them together. Since the fluorescence intensity of GSH-Au NCs at 606 nm changed more significantly than that at 642 nm, in this CDs/GSH-Au NCs system the ratio of the fluorescence intensity of GSH-Au NCs at 606 nm and CDs at 459 nm defined as I606/I459 was adopted for quantification in the following analysis.

In this system, the complex of CDs/GSH-Au NCs was used as the fluorescence probe, and La3+ served as an effective multi-dentate ligand to coordinate with GSH and FEN. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , in the La3+ containing CDs/GSH-Au NCs solution, the GSH-Au NCs were aggregated via the coordination between La3+ and GSH on the surface of GSH-Au NCs, accompanying an AIEE effect of GSH-Au NCs and a drastic enhanced fluorescence intensity of GSH-Au NCs. When FEN was added into this detection system, La3+could coordinate with thiophosphate of FEN. In the structure of FEN, both oxygen and sulfur atoms are likely to bind with La3+ due to their electronegative property. However, according to the principle of hard and soft acid and base, La3+ is a typical hard acid, and oxygen and sulfur atoms belong to hard base and soft base, respectively [38]. Thus La3+ exhibits a high affinity to oxygen-containing functional groups [39,40]. Consequently, La3+ is supposed to preferentially bind with oxygen atoms of thiophosphate in the present system as proposed in Scheme 1. The formation of La3+ centered polydentate complexes containing combinations of GSH and FEN may induce more and more Au NCs to gather together, thereby the secondary enhancement of fluorescence intensity of Au NCs was dependent on the concentration of FEN.

The proposed sensing mechanism was further confirmed by the fluorescence analysis and HRTEM of CDs/GSH-Au NCs in Fig. 2 . As shown in Fig. 2A, adding FEN alone was found to have almost no influence on the fluorescence of complex nanosystem (curve a, b), while adding La3+ generated an obvious increase of fluorescence intensity of GSH-Au NCs at 606 nm (curve a, c). Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of CDs/GSH-Au NCs at 606 nm further increased dramatically after adding FEN in the La3+ containing complex nanosystem (curve d). The HRTEM images showed that GSH-Au NCs were in monodisperse state in the absence of La3+ (Fig. 2B), while it presented as a spheric cluster with the addition of La3+ (Fig. 2C), which confirmed the aggregation of GSH-Au NCs induced by La3+. As displayed in Fig. 2D, a certain amount of spheric clusters would adhere together to form an irregular chain with the subsequent addition of FEN in the La3+ containing nanosystem, indicating the aggregation of GSH-Au NCs was exacerbated in the presence of FEN. Therefore, the quantitative analysis of FEN was mainly dependent on the subsequent increment of fluorescence intensity in this sensing system.

In a ratiometric fluorescence sensing system, it is necessary to mix Au NCs and CDs in a fixed ratio to fabricate a suitable ratio fluorescent probe [41]. In our experiment, the volume ratio of GSH-Au NCs to CDs in the detection system was fixed at 80:35 (v/v) because a distinguishable luminous variation under ultraviolet lamp (λ = 365 nm) could be observed in the presence of FEN with a higher concentration. To obtain good sensitivity for FEN detection, the amount of acetone, the concentration of La3+, pH value of FEN solution and reaction time were optimized. The fluorescence enhancement degree of GSH-Au NCs caused by FEN was calculated by (I–I1)/(I–I0) and used as an index for evaluating the sensing performance, where I0 and I1 and I represented the fluorescence intensity at 606 nm of CDs/GSH-Au NCs, La3+ containing CDs/GSH-Au NCs and CDs/GSH-Au NCs coexisting with La3+ and FEN, respectively.

Studies have shown that organic solvents can destroy the hydration shell of Au(I)-thiolate complexes and thus regulate the AIEE property of thiolate-protected GSH-Au NCs due to the aggregation of Au NCs [17,31], thus the volume of acetone in detection system was first optimized. For fluorescence analysis, the added FEN was kept at a concentration of 0.40 μg mL−1 with acetone varied from 250 μL to 500 μL, and La3+ was at a concentration of 4 μmol L−1 in the detection system (i.e. adding 50 μL of 160 μmol L−1 La3+). As displayed in Fig. 3 A, the (I–I1)/(I–I0) value attained a maximum in 300 μL acetone, and it decreased gradually with further increasing acetone volume fraction. This is reasonable in respect of the solvent induced aggregation effect of acetone, because a high fraction of acetone may contribute to a high fluorescence background of the nanosystem before the addition of FEN. Therefore, the volume of acetone was fixed at 300 μL in this sensing system.

Since La3+ not only resulted in a high fluorescence background of GSH-Au NCs but also served as a bridge of FEN and GSH-Au NCs, the effect of concentration of La3+ on fluorescence analysis was investigated with a fixed concentration of FEN (0.40 μg mL−1). As exhibited in Fig. 3B, the (I–I1)/(I–I0) value moved upward quickly with increasing La3+ concentration at the initial stage, and reached the highest point when La3+ was at a concentration of 4 μmol L−1 in the system, and then kept declining when the concentration of La3+ exceeded 4 μmol L−1. In principle, an excessive amount of free La3+ would consume the added FEN inevitably via coordination rather than acted as a bridge to bind FEN and GSH-Au NCs. Thus, a declined (I–I1)/(I–I0) value was observed successively. That is, more free La3+ are supposed to match with more FEN thereby decreasing the sensitivity for detecting FEN. Therefore, the optimal concentration of La3+ in detection system was 4 μmol L−1.

The influence of pH value of FEN solution on (I–I1)/(I–I0) value of detection system was also investigated considering that the coordination between La3+ and FEN is subjected to pH environment. A maximum value of (I–I1)/(I–I0) was found at pH 4.5 for FEN solution (Fig. 3C). It is reasonable because the oxygen atoms of thiophosphate in FEN are prone to be protonated when exposed to a strong acid medium for a long time, so La3+ would hardly coordinate with thiophosphate at a lower pH due to the acid effect [42]. It could also be observed that the fluorescence enhancement effect of GSH-Au NCs induced by La3+ was very weak at pH ≥ 6 (Fig. S2), which may attribute to the hydrolysis of both La3+ and FEN under alkaline conditions. On the one hand, the formation of hydroxides such as [La(OH)]2+, [La(OH)2]+, La(OH)3 and [La(OH)4]- upon increasing pH value in the solution occurred [43,44]. On the other hand, the hydroxyl groups as strong nucleophiles will replace –OR in FEN by attacking P atom and then partially hydrolyze to an ineffective ligand PS(OH)3 for La3+ [42]. Therefore, the pH value of FEN solution was adjusted to 4.5 when conducting fluorescence testing.

Based on the optimized experimental conditions as above, the reaction time of La3+ with FEN and GSH-Au NCs was studied. As depicted in Fig. 3D, the (I–I1)/(I–I0) value increased quickly at the beginning and then reached a balance when La3+ was incubated with FEN and GSH-Au NCs for 30 min. As a result, the determination of FEN was conducted at pH 4.5 for 30 min using CDs/GSH-Au NCs (35:80, v/v) containing 4 μmol L−1 La3+ and 300 μL acetone as sensing probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The calibration curve for quantitative analysis of FEN
ce:section.ce:para: The La3+ containing CDs/GSH-Au NCs fluorescence probe was used for determining FEN at the optimal conditions as described above. Fig. 4 A depicted the fluorescence emission spectra of the as-proposed probe with the addition of various concentrations of FEN. The fluorescence intensity at 606 nm gradually increased and the fluorescence intensity at 459 nm remained constant upon increasing the concentration of FEN solution. A good linear relationship (R2 = 0.992) was found between the value of I606/I459 and the concentration of FEN from 0.01 to 1.10 μg mL−1 (Fig. 4B). (I606 and I459 represent the fluorescence intensity of this complex probe at 606 nm and 459 nm, respectively.) The LOD (3 S/k) was determined to be 4.49 ng mL−1. In the present work, 15 mL extracts were obtained from 10 g apple, orange and cucumber samples, respectively, and around 2 mL (1835 μL) extracts were used for fluorescence analysis. So the lowest detectable concentration in real sample is required to be 6.74 ng g−1 using our developed method, which is much lower than the maximum allowable residue value of FEN regulated by Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs of China (50 ng g−1) [6].

To assess the selectivity of the as-developed fluorescence assay towards FEN, various common metal ions in irrigation water such as K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, and two germicides (chlorothalonil, vinclozolin) and two insecticides (bifenthrin, fenpropathrin) were selected for conducting competitive experiments under the optimal experimental conditions. The four pesticides are broad-spectrum pesticides used for treating fungal diseases and controlling pest populations of fruits and vegetables, which may remain in fruits and vegetables, to interfere with the detection of FEN. In addition, as exhibited in Scheme 1, the detection mechanism lies in the binding between La3+ and oxygen atoms of FEN. Therefore, the influence of the pesticides containing electron rich atoms (N and O) in the structure (Fig. S3) should be investigated [45,46]. The influences of individual interferent and the mixture of FEN and an individual interferent on the I606/I459 value of the probe were investigated, and the concentrations of metal ions and pesticides were 10-fold and 5-fold of that of FEN, respectively. As displayed in Fig. 5 , all of these disrupting substances generated a negligible change on the I606/I459 value, demonstrating that the as-proposed fluorescence method has excellent anti-interference ability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application for the detection of FEN in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the practicability of the as-proposed fluorescence method, the standard addition method was used for analyzing the content of FEN in apple, orange and cucumber samples. No detectable FEN was found in apple, orange and cucumber samples, so FEN solutions with several levels were spiked into the blank samples as described in section 2.5, and the recoveries were calculated using the established calibration curve and listed in Table 1 . The recoveries were 86.7%–102.7% for apple sample, 93.3%–99.6% for orange sample and 86.7%–97.6% for cucumber sample respectively, and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) were lower than 5.8%, demonstrating that the La3+ assisted CDs/GSH-Au NCs non-enzymatic probe was applicable for monitoring FEN in agro-products samples.

Table S1 summarized the analytical performance between this method and other reported methods for determining FEN in different sample matrices. The as-proposed fluorescence method exhibited comparable LOD and linear range as compared to other reported methods. Moreover, La3+ involved non-enzymatic ratiometric fluorescence method should be desirable for detection of FEN in various food samples as far as the low-cost, easy operation as well as straightforward visual signals concerned.

In conclusion, a non-enzymatic ratiometric fluorescence sensing method was developed for monitoring FEN residues in agro-products samples using La3+ assisted CDs/GSH-Au NCs as fluorescence probe. The sensing mechanism lies in successively taking advantage of the AIEE property of GSH-Au NCs assisted by La3+, in which the CDs fluorescence was used as the reference signal to fabricate a ratiometric fluorescence probe. The first increase of fluorescence intensity of GSH-Au NCs probe results from its aggregation caused by the coordination between La3+ and GSH capped on the surface of Au NCs, and the fluorescence enhancement of GSH-Au NCs probe is strengthened with an aggregated aggregation because both FEN and GSH are chelated with La3+. Quantitative detection of FEN is achieved according to the linear relationship between the ratio of fluorescence intensity of GSH-Au NCs and CDs (I606/I459) and the concentration of FEN. The La3+ assisted non-enzymatic fluorescence assay demonstrates quite good performance in terms of superior repeatability, sensitivity, selectivity towards FEN detection, which was successfully applied for the quantitative analysis of FEN in apple, orange and cucumber matrices with good recoveries ranging from 86.7% to 102.7%. The present work offers a facile and cost-effective strategy for monitoring FEN residues in farm products.

Mengting Guan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Haibo He: Conceptualization, Validation, Software, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Ruxing Li: Methodology, Data curation. Xiaojing Si: Instrumental test, Data curation. Xitian Peng: Resources, Instrumental test. Xiaoxia Yan: Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Zhaosheng Yang: Instrumental test, Data curation. Enhsuan Nien: Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Yunyi Lei: Resources, Instrumental test. Liqiang Luo: Resources, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [grant numbers 21974085, 61971274].
